title: Display & Edit Methods in X++
summary: Master display and edit methods to create calculated fields, custom UI behaviors, and data transformations in D365 F&O forms and reports.

content.overview:
Display and edit methods are one of X++'s most powerful features for creating calculated fields and custom UI behaviors without modifying base application tables. These methods execute on-demand when forms render or reports generate, allowing you to present transformed data, perform calculations, or control field editability based on runtime conditions. Unlike stored database fields, display/edit methods are computed in real-time, making them perfect for derived values, status indicators, and conditional formatting.

In D365 F&O architecture, display methods are read-only calculated fields that return values for display purposes, while edit methods provide two-way data binding for complex field interactions. They're essential for customizations that need to present data differently than it's stored, implement business logic for field validation, or create composite fields that combine multiple table fields. Understanding their execution context, caching behavior, and performance implications is crucial for building responsive, maintainable solutions.
content.theory:
Display and edit methods are declared with the [Display] and [Edit] attributes respectively, and must follow specific naming and signature conventions. The framework automatically discovers these methods through reflection and binds them to form controls or report fields based on the Extended Data Type (EDT) specified in the attribute. The method execution occurs within the table's context, giving you access to all table fields and methods through the 'this' reference.

The caching mechanism is critical to understand: display methods are cached per record until the record's data changes or the cache is explicitly invalidated using the table's reread() method. This prevents redundant calculations but can cause stale data if external dependencies change. The SysTableBrowser class and form data sources handle this caching automatically, but custom scenarios may require manual cache management.

For form integration, display/edit methods bound to form controls automatically participate in the form's data binding lifecycle. The FormDataSource calls these methods during record navigation, refresh operations, and field validation. Edit methods receive the new value as a parameter and must handle validation, transformation, and persistence to underlying table fields. They should call the table's validateField() method to trigger standard validation frameworks.

code_examples.title: Basic Display Method with Business Logic
code_examples.description: A display method on CustTable that calculates customer credit utilization percentage with proper error handling and null checking

code_examples.title: Advanced Edit Method with Validation
code_examples.description: An edit method that handles complex field interactions, validation, and automatic field updates when dependencies change

code_examples.title: Performance-Optimized Display Method with Caching
code_examples.description: A complex display method that implements custom caching and batch data retrieval to avoid N+1 query problems

key_points: Display methods are read-only and execute on-demand — they should never modify table data directly, only return calculated or transformed values for presentation
Edit methods provide bidirectional data binding — the boolean parameter indicates if it's a get (_set=false) or set (_set=true) operation, and you must handle both scenarios
Method signatures must match exact patterns — display methods return the EDT type and take no parameters, edit methods return EDT type and take boolean + value parameters
Caching behavior is automatic but controllable — the framework caches results per record until data changes, but you can force refresh with table.reread() or implement custom caching
Performance implications are significant — display methods execute for every visible record in grids, so optimize queries and consider batch loading for expensive operations
gotchas: Display methods in grids execute for EVERY visible row — if your grid shows 50 records and your display method does a database query, that's 50 queries. Use static caching or preload data to avoid N+1 problems
Edit method validation exceptions don't automatically rollback — wrap database modifications in ttsbegin/ttscommit blocks and handle rollback explicitly in catch blocks to maintain data consistency

Recursive display method calls cause stack overflow — if display method A calls a method that triggers display method B, which calls back to A, you'll crash. Use static flags or redesign the dependency chain
performance_tips: Implement static caching for expensive display methods that show the same data across multiple records — use Maps with cache expiry logic to balance performance and data freshness
Use batch preloading in form initialization — call a static method that loads all required data in a single query, then cache results for individual display method calls

quiz.q: What happens when a display method is bound to a grid column showing 100 records?
options: The method executes once and the result is shared
The method executes 100 times, once per record
The method executes only for visible rows on screen
The method is cached and executes only when data changes
quiz.explanation: Display methods execute once for each record in the grid, which can cause performance issues. This is why caching and batch preloading are crucial for expensive operations.

quiz.q: In an edit method, how do you determine if you should return the current value or set a new value?
options: Check if the second parameter is null
Check the boolean _set parameter
Check if the method was called from a form
Check the table's current state
quiz.explanation: Edit methods use the boolean _set parameter to determine the operation mode: false means get/return current value, true means set/validate the new value provided in the second parameter.

quiz.q: What is the best way to handle database queries in display methods for optimal performance?
options: Use direct SQL queries instead of X++ select statements
Implement static caching with expiry logic
Always use firstonly in select statements
Call the display method recursively to build results
quiz.explanation: Static caching with expiry logic is the best approach because it prevents repeated database queries for the same data while ensuring data freshness through cache invalidation strategies.

title: Delegates & Event-Driven X++
summary: Master X++ delegates and event-driven programming to build loosely-coupled, extensible business logic in D365 F&O.

content.overview:
Delegates in X++ are type-safe function pointers that enable event-driven programming and the Observer pattern in D365 F&O. Unlike traditional inheritance-based extensibility, delegates provide a publish-subscribe mechanism where multiple subscribers can react to business events without tight coupling to the publisher. This is crucial for ISV solutions, customizations, and integrations that need to respond to standard business processes without modifying core Microsoft code.

Delegates are extensively used throughout D365 F&O's standard application, from form events (FormDataSourceEventArgs) to business logic events in classes like SalesTable and PurchTable. Understanding delegates is essential for modern X++ development, as they're the foundation for Chain of Command (CoC) extensibility and custom business event frameworks.

While delegates add a layer of indirection that can impact performance if misused, they're invaluable for building maintainable, loosely-coupled solutions that survive application updates and enable multiple customizations to coexist peacefully.
content.theory:
X++ delegates are implemented as special AOT objects under the \Classes node with the 'delegate' keyword. They define a contract (method signature) that subscriber methods must implement. The runtime maintains a subscription list for each delegate instance, invoking all subscribers sequentially when the delegate is called. This follows the multicast delegate pattern from .NET but with X++ semantics.

The D365 F&O framework uses delegates extensively in the Application Platform layer. For example, FormDataSource uses delegates like 'validatingWrite' and 'written' to allow customizations to participate in form data operations. The SysExtensionFramework leverages delegates for plugin architectures, and the Business Events framework uses delegates to publish integration events.

Delegates in X++ support both static and instance methods as subscribers. Static delegates are defined at the class level and share subscription lists across all instances, while instance delegates maintain per-object subscription lists. The subscription mechanism uses weak references to prevent memory leaks when subscriber objects are garbage collected. Understanding this distinction is crucial for avoiding subscription lifecycle issues in complex business scenarios.

code_examples.title: Basic Delegate Definition and Usage
code_examples.description: Creating a delegate for sales order validation with multiple subscribers

code_examples.title: Advanced Delegate Pattern with Error Handling
code_examples.description: Production-grade delegate usage with subscription management and error isolation

code_examples.title: Form Delegate Integration
code_examples.description: Integrating delegates with form events for dynamic UI behavior

key_points: Delegates enable loose coupling by allowing multiple subscribers to react to events without knowing about each other or the publisher
Use += and -= operators to subscribe/unsubscribe from delegates — always clean up subscriptions to prevent memory leaks
Static delegates share subscription lists across all instances, while instance delegates maintain per-object subscriptions
Delegate calls are synchronous and sequential — if one subscriber throws an exception, subsequent subscribers won't execute
FormDataSourceEventArgs and FormEventArgs are built on delegates, making them the foundation of modern D365 F&O form extensibility
gotchas: Memory leaks from orphaned subscriptions — always unsubscribe in finalize() or when the subscriber object is no longer needed, especially for static delegates
Exception propagation stops delegate chain — wrap delegate calls in try/catch to prevent one failing subscriber from breaking others
Recursive delegate calls can cause stack overflow — implement isProcessing flags to detect and prevent recursive event scenarios
performance_tips: Minimize delegate subscriber count and complexity since all subscribers execute synchronously on each delegate call
Use conditional logic in subscribers to exit early when processing isn't needed for the current context or record state

quiz.q: What happens when one subscriber method throws an unhandled exception during delegate execution?
options: All subscribers continue to execute normally
Subsequent subscribers in the chain are not executed
Only the first subscriber is affected
The delegate automatically retries the failed subscriber
quiz.explanation: When a subscriber throws an unhandled exception, the delegate call chain stops and subsequent subscribers are not executed. This is why production code should wrap delegate calls in try/catch blocks to isolate subscriber failures.

quiz.q: How do static delegates differ from instance delegates in terms of subscription management?
options: Static delegates maintain per-object subscription lists
Static delegates share subscription lists across all class instances
Instance delegates are faster than static delegates
There is no difference between static and instance delegates
quiz.explanation: Static delegates maintain a single, shared subscription list across all instances of the class, while instance delegates maintain separate subscription lists for each object instance. This affects memory management and subscription lifecycle.

quiz.q: Which operator is used to unsubscribe from a delegate in X++?
options: remove()
unsubscribe()
quiz.explanation: The -= operator is used to unsubscribe from delegates in X++, just like += is used to subscribe. This follows the same pattern as C# multicast delegate syntax.

title: Cross-Company Queries in X++
summary: Master cross-company data operations in D365 F&O using crossCompany, changeCompany, and advanced patterns for multi-entity scenarios.

content.overview: Cross-company queries are fundamental for multi-entity D365 F&O implementations where data must be accessed across legal entities. Unlike traditional single-company operations, cross-company scenarios require careful handling of company context, security boundaries, and performance considerations. D365 F&O provides several mechanisms including the crossCompany keyword, changeCompany statement, and Query framework extensions that enable developers to traverse company boundaries safely and efficiently. Understanding these patterns is crucial for enterprise implementations where consolidated reporting, inter-company transactions, and shared master data scenarios are common. The framework automatically handles company filtering in most cases, but cross-company operations require explicit developer intervention and proper security model implementation.
content.theory: D365 F&O's multi-company architecture is built on the concept of DataAreaId, which acts as a partition key for most transactional tables. The kernel maintains company context through the curext() function and CompanyInfo singleton, ensuring data isolation by default. Cross-company operations bypass this isolation through three primary mechanisms: the crossCompany keyword in select statements, the changeCompany statement for context switching, and the Query class's allowCrossCompany() method. The SysQuery framework provides additional cross-company capabilities through CrossCompanyQueryUtils and the QueryBuildDataSource.allowCrossCompany() method. Security is enforced through the XDSSecurityPolicy framework, which can restrict cross-company access based on user permissions and table-level security policies. Performance considerations include the fact that crossCompany queries cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId, forcing table scans in some scenarios. The RecordLevelSecurity framework also interacts with cross-company operations, requiring careful consideration of which companies a user can access through role-based security.

code_examples.title: Basic crossCompany Query with Error Handling
code_examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental cross-company query syntax with proper error handling and security validation

code_examples.title: Advanced Query Framework Cross-Company Pattern
code_examples.description: Production-grade cross-company implementation using Query framework with joins and complex filtering

code_examples.title: changeCompany Pattern for Cross-Company Updates
code_examples.description: Demonstrates safe cross-company data updates using changeCompany with transaction management

key_points: crossCompany keyword enables querying across multiple legal entities in a single select statement — use container of company IDs to limit scope
changeCompany statement switches execution context to another legal entity — essential for cross-company updates and maintaining data integrity
Query framework's allowCrossCompany() method provides programmatic control over cross-company operations with better performance for complex scenarios
Security validation must be performed explicitly for each target company using SecurityRights::hasTableAccess() before accessing cross-company data
Performance degrades significantly with crossCompany queries as DataAreaId-based indexes cannot be used — consider caching and limiting result sets
gotchas: crossCompany queries ignore company-specific customizations and extensions — CoC and event handlers may not fire as expected in target companies
Transaction boundaries with changeCompany can cause deadlocks — always use consistent company ordering and minimize transaction scope across companies
RecordLevelSecurity policies are evaluated in current company context, not target company — manually validate RLS rules when crossing company boundaries
performance_tips: Use specific company containers instead of querying all companies — CrossCompanyQueryUtils.getAllowedCompanies() respects user security but may return too many companies
Consider using SysOperation framework for long-running cross-company operations to avoid timeout issues and provide progress feedback to users

quiz.q: Which method is most efficient for updating the same record across multiple companies?
options: Multiple crossCompany select statements in a loop
Single changeCompany statement with company loop
Query framework with allowCrossCompany(true)
Separate batch jobs per company
quiz.explanation: changeCompany with a company loop is most efficient because it maintains proper transaction context, allows index usage within each company, and provides better error handling granularity compared to crossCompany select which cannot be used for updates.

quiz.q: What happens to RecordLevelSecurity when using crossCompany queries?
options: RLS policies are applied in each target company automatically
RLS is evaluated in the current company context only
RLS is completely bypassed for crossCompany operations
RLS policies must be manually specified in the query
quiz.explanation: RecordLevelSecurity policies are evaluated in the current company context only when using crossCompany queries. This means developers must manually validate security rules for each target company to ensure proper data access control.

quiz.q: Why do crossCompany queries typically have poor performance compared to single-company queries?
options: Network latency between companies
Additional security validation overhead
Cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId
Must load all company metadata first
quiz.explanation: crossCompany queries cannot use indexes that include DataAreaId as a key field, which forces table scans instead of efficient index seeks. Since most D365 tables include DataAreaId in their primary indexes, this significantly impacts query performance.
